mission.title: connect-rpc: More request information in Interceptor.WrapStreamContext()
mission.description: More request information in Interceptor.WrapStreamContext(). Requested by 1+ users.

steps.title: Check current connect-rpc deployment
steps.description:
Verify your connect-rpc version and configuration:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n connect-rpc -l app.kubernetes.io/name=connect-rpc
helm list -n connect-rpc 2>/dev/null || echo "Not installed via Helm"
```
This feature requires a working connect-rpc installation.

steps.title: Review connect-rpc configuration
steps.description:
Inspect the relevant connect-rpc configuration:
```bash
kubectl get all -n connect-rpc -l app.kubernetes.io/name=connect-rpc
kubectl get configmap -n connect-rpc -l app.kubernetes.io/part-of=connect-rpc
```
Currently there is no way to enrich a stream context with information about the request. The `WrapStreamContext()` method only accepts a parent context and does not give us any information about the request.

steps.title: Apply the fix for More request information in Interceptor.WrapStreamContext()

impossible to write a panic-recovering interceptor. It also makes common

steps.title: Verify the feature works
steps.description:
Test that the new capability is working as expected:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n connect-rpc -l app.kubernetes.io/name=connect-rpc
kubectl get events -n connect-rpc --sort-by='.lastTimestamp' | tail -10
```
Confirm the feature described in "More request information in Interceptor.WrapStreamContext()" is functioning correctly.

resolution.summary:
Streaming interceptors aren't very useful in their current state. Rather
than wrapping user code, they wrap the the networking primitives used by
the user's code. This is a subtle distinction, but it makes it
impossible to write a panic-recovering interceptor. It also makes common
server-side interceptors (e.g., authentication) needlessly difficult to
write.
